title: Weak wave turbulence as a precursor to universal coarsening in a homogeneous Bose gas
authors: Simon M. Fischer, Martin Gazo, Sebastian J. Morris, Nikolai Maslov, Haoyu Zhang, Jiří Etrych, Gevorg Martirosyan, Christoph Eigen, Zoran Hadzibabic
abstract: Relaxation and condensation of an isolated low-energy Bose gas provide an ideal setting for the study of the universal features of far-from-equilibrium many-body dynamics and the emergence of long-range order. Conceptually, the emergence of such order involves two steps: the formation of local coherence, on a system-specific microscopic lengthscale, and the spreading of coherence, over lengthscales much larger than any microscopic scale. The latter is understood in terms of universal phase-ordering kinetics, or coarsening, characterized by an algebraic growth of the coherence length. Here, for a homogeneous Bose gas with tunable interactions, we show that the former also has a universal description, within the framework of weak wave turbulence (WWT). Specifically, the initial transport of particles to low momenta corresponds to an inverse turbulent cascade that is, in agreement with the WWT theory, characterized by a power-law momentum distribution, with exponent $γ= 2.4(1)$, and transport times ${\propto} (na)^{-2}$, where $n$ is the gas density and $a$ the $s$-wave scattering length.
summary: This study experimentally demonstrates that the initial relaxation of a far-from-equilibrium Bose gas is governed by Weak Wave Turbulence. It shows that particle transport follows a universal power-law distribution and scaling laws that act as a precursor to the later stage of universal coarsening.
main_problem: Understanding the relaxation and condensation dynamics of a far-from-equilibrium, homogeneous Bose gas, specifically identifying the universal description of the initial stage of particle transport that precedes universal coarsening.
main_result: The researchers demonstrate that the initial stage of particle transport follows a universal Weak Wave Turbulence (WWT) description, characterized by a power-law momentum distribution with exponent gamma = 2.4(1) and transport times scaling as (na)^-2.
method: The study uses an experimental platform of 39K atoms in an optical box trap, evolving the gas by tuning the s-wave scattering length via Feshbach resonance and monitoring the time-dependent momentum distribution.
model_or_system: A homogeneous, low-energy, far-from-equilibrium Bose gas of 39K atoms held in a cylindrical optical box trap with tunable density and interaction strength.
key_observables: Spectral population density N_k(k), momentum distribution n_k, peak momentum k_p, and the exponent gamma.
important_parameters: Density n, s-wave scattering length a, healing length xi, and the WWT vs. coarsening regimes.
figures_summary: Fig 3(a) shows identical N_k/N curves for different n and a; Fig 3(b) plots transport time delta t versus na; Fig 4(a) shows the evolution of the gas momentum distribution.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of Bose gas relaxation, describes the experimental setup with 39K atoms, presents the observation of WWT scaling in momentum distributions and transport times, and concludes by showing the transition from WWT to the universal coarsening regime.
why_interesting: This paper provides a direct experimental link between the kinetic regime of weak wave turbulence and the late-time universal coarsening dynamics, offering a complete picture of non-equilibrium many-body relaxation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Momentum-space particle transport during far-from- equilibrium condensation in an isolated homogeneous Bose gas. The system starts (at time t = 0) in an incoherent low-energy state and condenses as it relaxes towards equilibrium. We show the evolution of the spectral population density Nk(k) = 4πk2nk(k), where nk is the momentum distribution; kp is the peak position of Nk, and kξ the inverse healing length. Here the gas density is n ≈2.8µm−3 and the scattering length a = 50a0, corresponding to kξ ≈0.43µm−1. (a) The condensate (spectral peak near k = 0) starts to form only after kp drops to ≈kξ. Here, we study the initial particle transport, to ≈kξ, and show that it corresponds to...

FIG. 2. Emergence of the WWT power-law spectrum. (a) Momentum distribution nk for various relaxation times, corresponding to different kp, for n ≈2.8µm−3 and a = 50a0, so kξ ≈0.43µm−1; k0 ≈1.9µm−1 is the initial value of kp. Once kp drops to ≈kξ, in the spectral range kξ ≲k ≲k0 we observe a power-law nk ∝k−γ, with γ ≈2.4 (red dashed line), close to theoretical predictions (see text). (b) Fitted γ values and the corresponding χ2 (with the dot-dashed line showing χ2 = 1) for fits in two different spectral ranges: from kξ to k0 (open symbols) and from the instantaneous kp to k0 (solid symbols). In the former case, the fitted γ approaches 2.4 (horizontal dashed line) as kp approaches kξ...

FIG. 3. Characteristic particle-transport time. For different n and a (as in Fig. 2), we extract the time ∆t(n,a) for kp to drop from k0 ≈1.9µm−1 to ≈k0/2. (a) Nk normalized by the total atom number N, for kp ≈k0/2 and different (n,a) (colored symbols), and for our initial state (gray); the dashed line indicates the largest kξ. The fact that, as kp decreases, the Nk/N curves for different (n,a) remain the same shows that ∆t(n,a) fully captures the de- pendence of the dynamics on the interactions. Importantly, ∆t is the same (within errors) for the two systems with the same product na (triangles). (b) Plotting ∆t versus na, we find ∆t ∝(na)−2.0(1)

FIG. 4. Connection to universal coarsening. In WWT, dk−2 p /dt is ∝(na)2 ∝k4 ξ. However, for kp ≪kξ, in the coarsening regime, it should be bounded by the universal speed limit, ≈0.37ℏ/m (see text), where m is the atom mass. (a) Here we first prepare a gas with n ≈3.7µm−3 and a = 90a0 in a state with kp ≈0.4µm−1, by evolving it from our initial state with kp ≈1.9µm−1, and then change a to different values in the range (7–240)a0, such that kξ varies from ≈0.19µm−1 to ≈1.1 µm−1 (dashed lines). (b) Ex- tracting dk−2 p /dt after the change of a and plotting it versus kp/kξ, we see how it bends away from the WWT scaling (dashed line) to- wards its speed limit (solid line).
